scotland.title: The Highland & Islay Malts
scotland.desc: Scotland is the undisputed home of single malt whisky. From the peat-drenched shores of Islay to the gentle river valleys of Speyside, each region imparts an unmistakable character shaped by centuries of craft, cold Atlantic winds, and pure highland water.

regions.name: Speyside
regions.desc: The heartland of Scotch. Home to more distilleries than any other region, producing whisky defined by orchard fruits, honey, and silky sherry notes.

regions.name: Islay
regions.desc: A small island with an outsized reputation. Islay malts are defined by medicinal peat smoke, brine, seaweed, and a ferocious maritime intensity.

regions.name: Highlands
regions.desc: The largest and most diverse whisky region. From fruity and floral to full-bodied and smoky, the Highlands defy easy categorisation.

timeline.event: First written record of Scotch whisky
timeline.desc: A Scottish Exchequer Roll references "aqua vitae" made by Friar John Cor — the earliest documented mention of Scotch.
The Act made legal distilling commercially viable and ended the era of illicit stills, ushering in the modern Scotch industry.
Scotch whisky must be aged a minimum of three years in oak — a rule that still defines the category today.
Five geographical categories — Single Malt, Single Grain, Blended Malt, Blended Grain, and Blended — receive legal protection.

france.title: Cognac & the Art of
france.desc: France elevates spirits to high art. The Charente region's Grande Champagne chalky soils produce eaux-de-vie of extraordinary delicacy, while the rugged hills of Gascony give Armagnac its wild, rustic soul. Two regions, two philosophies — both magnificent.

regions.name: Grande Champagne
regions.desc: The most prized cognac terroir. Chalky limestone soils produce eaux-de-vie of exceptional finesse, floral elegance and extraordinary aging potential.

regions.name: Gascony
regions.desc: France's oldest brandy region. Armagnac is distilled once in a continuous still, preserving more of the raw grape character — earthy, rustic, wild.

regions.name: Bordeaux
regions.desc: The world's most prestigious wine region also contributes rare aged brandies and a culture of terroir-driven production that permeates every bottle.

timeline.desc: Gascon alchemists produce the first Armagnac, predating Cognac by centuries — making it France's oldest spirit.

timeline.event: Cognac trade begins with Dutch merchants
timeline.desc: Dutch traders distill Charente wines to preserve them for export — "brandewijn" (burnt wine) gives brandy its name.
Maurice Hennessy creates the term "Extra Old" to describe a special reserve blend — a classification still used globally.
French law delimits the six cognac crus, protecting the geographic origin of one of the world's most imitated spirits.

japan.title: Precision and the
japan.desc: Japanese whisky emerged from a desire to perfect Scottish traditions through the lens of Japanese craftsmanship — shokunin spirit applied to distillation. The result is a category defined by extraordinary balance, delicacy, and complexity that the world has come to revere.

regions.name: Yamazaki
regions.desc: Japan's oldest distillery, nestled at the confluence of three rivers. Humid conditions and diverse cask types produce whisky of extraordinary complexity.

regions.name: Yoichi
regions.desc: Set on the cold, rugged coast of Hokkaido, Yoichi produces Japan's most robust and peated whiskies — inspired by Masataka Taketsuru's training in Scotland.

regions.name: Hakushu
regions.desc: A "forest distillery" at 700m altitude in the Japanese Alps. Hakushu's whiskies are clean, herbal, and gently peated with a distinctive freshness.

timeline.desc: Shinjiro Torii founds Suntory's Yamazaki Distillery, beginning Japan's whisky journey. Masataka Taketsuru serves as head distiller.
Taketsuru returns to Hokkaido, founding Nikka and creating a second pillar of Japan's whisky heritage.

timeline.event: Nikka wins Best of the Best at IWSC
timeline.desc: A landmark moment — Japan announces itself to the global whisky community. International accolades follow in rapid succession.
Global demand surges beyond supply. Age-stated expressions become rare; NAS releases rise. Prices soar internationally.

mexico.title: Fire, Earth &
mexico.desc: Mexico gives the world two of its most distinctive spirits — Tequila from the blue agave fields of Jalisco, and Mezcal from the ancient hand-crafted traditions of Oaxaca. Both are expressions of terroir as profound as any wine or whisky.

regions.name: Jalisco
regions.desc: The volcanic lowlands and highland valleys around Guadalajara define tequila. Blue Weber agave grown in red volcanic soil produces spirits of remarkable range.

regions.name: Oaxaca
regions.desc: Mezcal's ancient homeland. Over 30 agave varieties are used by maestros mezcaleros, roasted in earthen pits to produce smoky, complex spirits of breathtaking variety.

regions.name: Guerrero
regions.desc: An emerging region producing Raicilla — a lesser-known agave spirit of wild, coastal character. Guerrero distillers are among Mexico's most experimental.

timeline.desc: Indigenous Mexicans ferment the sap of the maguey agave to create pulque, a sacred ritual drink long before distillation arrived.
Spanish conquistadors introduce copper pot stills, leading to the first distilled agave spirits — the precursor to mezcal and tequila.

timeline.event: Jose Cuervo receives first tequila licence
timeline.desc: The Royal Governor of New Galicia grants Jose Cuervo the first licence to commercially produce tequila — an industry is born.
The Denomination of Origin for Tequila restricts production to Jalisco and parts of four other states, protecting the category globally.

ireland.title: Triple Distilled &
ireland.desc: Ireland's whiskey tradition is the world's oldest, with a style defined by triple distillation in large copper pot stills, producing spirits of exceptional smoothness, lightness, and a characteristic honeyed sweetness that sets them apart from all others.

regions.name: Dublin
regions.desc: The historic home of Irish whiskey, where Jameson and Teeling carry the city's tradition of smooth, approachable pot still expressions.

regions.name: Cork
regions.desc: Midleton Distillery, the world's largest pot still producer, crafts Jameson, Redbreast, and Green Spot — the cornerstones of the Irish style.

regions.name: Cooley Peninsula
regions.desc: Ireland's first independent distillery, founded in 1987. Cooley brought diversity back to Irish whiskey with peated expressions and single malts.

timeline.desc: The Annals of Clonmacnoise record the death of a chieftain from "taking a surfeit of aqua vitae" — the first written Irish whiskey reference.
The Old Midleton Distillery is established in Cork — later to become the spiritual home of Irish pot still whiskey.
The great consolidation. Jameson, Powers, and Cork Distilleries merge, preserving Irish whiskey during its most challenging period.
Over 30 new distilleries open across Ireland, reversing a century of decline. Irish whiskey becomes the world's fastest-growing spirit category.

caribbean.title: Sun-Kissed &
caribbean.desc: The Caribbean archipelago is the cradle of rum. From the full-bodied pot-still rums of Jamaica to the lighter column-still rums of Barbados and the sugarcane paradise of Martinique, the islands offer a diversity of styles unlike any other spirit category.

regions.name: Jamaica
regions.desc: Jamaica's rums are the most characterful in the world — high-ester, funky pot-still spirits from venerable houses like Appleton and Hampden.

regions.name: Martinique
regions.desc: The only rum with a Protected Designation of Origin. Made from fresh sugarcane juice rather than molasses, Martinique's Rhum Agricole has a grassy, vegetal terroir.

regions.name: Barbados
regions.desc: The birthplace of rum. Barbados produces lighter, refined expressions — most notably Mount Gay and Foursquare, celebrated for their elegant oak integration.

timeline.desc: Plantation slaves in Barbados discover that molasses, a byproduct of sugar production, can be fermented and distilled into a potent spirit.
The oldest continually operating rum distillery in the world is founded in Barbados — its production records survive to this day.
French colonists in Martinique begin distilling fresh cane juice rather than molasses — creating a completely different, greener style of rum.
Martinique Rhum Agricole receives its Appellation d'Origine Contrôlée — the only rum in the world with this protected French designation.

usa.title: Bourbon & the
usa.desc: The United States gave the world Bourbon — a spirit so tied to its homeland that by law it can only be called Bourbon if made in America. The charred new oak barrels, the rich corn mash, and Kentucky's limestone-filtered water produce a distinctly American vision of whiskey.

regions.name: Kentucky
regions.desc: 95% of the world's Bourbon is aged in Kentucky. The state's climate — hot summers, cold winters — forces whiskey in and out of the charred barrel wood, extracting rich flavour rapidly.

regions.name: Tennessee
regions.desc: Tennessee whiskey takes one extra step: the Lincoln County Process filters new-make spirit through sugar maple charcoal before barrel entry, producing a distinctly mellow character.

regions.name: Texas
regions.desc: Texas's extreme heat accelerates maturation dramatically. What takes Kentucky 10 years, Texas achieves in 3 — producing bold, oak-forward expressions with a frontier spirit.

distInfo.v: New charred American white oak only

timeline.desc: With Scotch-Irish settlers distilling grain spirits in the new frontier, American whiskey takes root as the spirit of the young republic.
The county that gives Bourbon its name is founded. Distillers in the region begin shipping aged corn whiskey downstream to New Orleans.
The first US consumer protection law for spirits guarantees that Bonded Bourbon is at least 4 years old, 100 proof, and from one distillery.

timeline.event: Bourbon declared a "distinctive product of the USA"
timeline.desc: A Congressional resolution protects Bourbon as an American-made spirit — a designation that remains legally binding to this day.
